CFD Solution Verification · Code-to-Benchmark · interactive

Laminar Vortex Shedding behind a Circular Cylinder at Re = 100

OpenFOAM pimpleFoam (v2312) · incompressible transient laminar · body-fitted O-grid · digitalmodel CFD capability (issue #1166, epic #1161)

VERIFIED — C̅d within 0.5% and St within 0.9% of benchmark

SolverOpenFOAM pimpleFoam
Reynolds No.Re = 100
Mesh21,120 cells (O-grid)
Mean Cd1.344
Strouhal St0.1655
Cl amplitude0.325

1  Objective

Verify the digitalmodel transient-CFD pipeline against the canonical laminar Karman vortex street behind a circular cylinder at Reynolds number 100 — the second foundational known-answer case of the CFD validation hierarchy (after the flat-plate boundary layer). The two target quantities have well-established experimental and computational benchmark values:

mean drag  C̅d ≈ 1.33–1.37   Strouhal  St = fsD/U∞ ≈ 0.164

where fs is the vortex-shedding frequency, D the cylinder diameter and U∞ the free-stream speed.

What this gates in real work — VIV screening

Alternating vortex shedding is the forcing behind vortex-induced vibration of risers, jacket members, mooring lines and pipeline free spans. This case verifies the two numbers VIV screening depends on: the shedding frequency (Strouhal, +0.9%) and the force amplitude (Cd −0.5%, Ĉl in the published band). Try it on a real member:

Shedding freq fₛ = St·U/D—
Reduced velocity Uₙ = U/(fₙD)—
Lock-in screen (4 ≤ Uₙ ≤ 8)—

Uses the sub-critical Strouhal St ≈ 0.2 (10³ < Re < 2×10⁵), the flat part of the St–Re curve; this verification case sits at Re = 100 where St = 0.164–0.166, the laminar benchmark point. Lock-in band per DNV-RP-C205 / DNV-RP-F105 screening practice; a hit means fatigue assessment (e.g. the free-span workflow) is required, not that failure occurs.

2  Result — the vortex street

3  Numerical model

ItemSpecification
SolverpimpleFoam — transient incompressible PIMPLE, laminar closure
MeshBody-fitted O-grid, 21,120 cells; cylinder D=1, circular far-field at 20 D; radial wall-clustering. Non-orthogonality <0.001, max skewness 0.03
FlowU∞=1, ν=0.01 → Re = U∞D/ν = 100 (laminar shedding regime)
Boundary conditionscylinder noSlip; far-field freestream/freestreamPressure (entraining)
Discretisation2nd-order: backward time, Gauss linear convection; adjustable Δt at Co≤5
Runt = 0→160; forces sampled over the saturated limit cycle t = 120–160 (5 shedding periods)

4  Triggering the instability (engineering note)

Why the wake first refused to shed. On a perfectly grid-aligned, symmetric setup the discrete solution preserves the up/down symmetry, so the shedding instability — though physically unstable at Re=100 (above the critical Re≈47) — cannot grow from a symmetric state; the lift stayed identically zero to t>40. Because a circle is rotationally symmetric, tilting the free stream by 5° is the same physical flow rotated — it leaves Cd and St unchanged — but it breaks the grid-aligned numerical symmetry and seeds the instability, which then grows exponentially and saturates by t≈120. Forces are measured along the rotated flow/normal axes, so the reported coefficients are unbiased.

5  Force history & quantitative agreement
